coredata.dc:description: Protein derived carbon quantum dots (PCQDs) were successfully fabricated via a hydrothermal method with shrimp meat as the precursor. Subsequently, their corrosion-inhibiting performance on mild steel (L-steel) in a 0.5 mol/L H2SO4 solution was evaluated. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests demonstrated that at a concentration of 250 mg/L, the corrosion inhibition efficiency of PCQDs exceeded 90 %. Potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) tests indicated that when the concentration of PCQDs reached 500 mg/L, the corrosion inhibition efficiency was as high as 97.45 %. Comprehensive characterizations of the PCQDs, including X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), verified the existence of functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and aromatic rings within the PCQDs. These groups are conducive to forming a strong adsorption force on the surface of L-steel and creating a protective layer. Surface morphology analyses carried out by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) revealed that the surface of L-steel treated with PCQDs was significantly smoother in comparison to untreated samples exposed to acidic conditions. Adsorption studies disclosed a mixed physical and chemical adsorption behavior, and theoretical calculations showed that the N and S atoms in PCQDs preferentially adsorb in a parallel direction. The addition of PCQDs led to a reduction in the diffusion coefficient, indicating that the diffusion of corrosive ions was effectively hindered. The distribution function of PCQDs was 3.48 Å, which suggested that the chemisorption mechanism played a dominant role in the inhibition process.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Protein derived carbon quantum dots (PCQDs) were successfully fabricated via a hydrothermal method with shrimp meat as the precursor. Subsequently, their corrosion-inhibiting performance on mild steel (L-steel) in a 0.5 mol/L H2SO4 solution was evaluated. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests demonstrated that at a concentration of 250 mg/L, the corrosion inhibition efficiency of PCQDs exceeded 90 %. Potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) tests indicated that when the concentration of PCQDs reached 500 mg/L, the corrosion inhibition efficiency was as high as 97.45 %. Comprehensive characterizations of the PCQDs, including X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), verified the existence of functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and aromatic rings within the PCQDs. These groups are conducive to forming a strong adsorption force on the surface of L-steel and creating a protective layer. Surface morphology analyses carried out by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) revealed that the surface of L-steel treated with PCQDs was significantly smoother in comparison to untreated samples exposed to acidic conditions. Adsorption studies disclosed a mixed physical and chemical adsorption behavior, and theoretical calculations showed that the N and S atoms in PCQDs preferentially adsorb in a parallel direction. The addition of PCQDs led to a reduction in the diffusion coefficient, indicating that the diffusion of corrosive ions was effectively hindered. The distribution function of PCQDs was 3.48 Å, which suggested that the chemisorption mechanism played a dominant role in the inhibition process.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal corrosion is a global concern, which not only affects the safety and reliability of equipment, but also brings serious environmental pollution and economic losses [1]. Low carbon steel (L-steel), as the most commonly used metal material in industrial production, is widely used in construction, machinery manufacturing, piping and containers due to its low price and excellent mechanical properties [2]. However, L-steel is susceptible to a variety of environmental factors such as humidity, O2, salt, pH and contaminants in actual use, leading to corrosion [3,4]. About 30 % of the steel produced each year will fail due to corrosion. Metal corrosion not only shortens the service life of the material, but also brings huge economic losses to the enterprise and may even lead to major safety accidents [5]. When corrosion occurs in metal pipelines or equipment, the gradual damage to its equipment and structure leads to a series of problems such as leakage and environmental pollution.

Corrosion inhibitors (CIs) have broad application prospects in metallurgy, machinery, transportation, petroleum and other industries due to their low dosage, easy operation, good economy and wide range of adaptability [6]. CIs extend the service life of metallic materials by forming a protective film on the surface of L-steel and inhibiting the contact of corrosive media with the metal. According to their chemical composition, CIs can be divided into two categories: organic and inorganic [7–9]. However, many traditional inorganic CIs such as chromates, silicates, molybdates, tungstates, etc., although they have excellent inhibition effect, there is large toxicity, biodegradation difficulties, easy to cause environmental pollution, and it is difficult to achieve sustainable development [10]. Organic CIs include phosphate, benzotriazole, sulfonated lignin and some other nitrogen oxide-containing heterocyclic compounds, etc., primarily function by adsorbing onto metal surfaces, forming a protective layer that inhibits corrosion [11]. These organic CIs, although relatively environmentally friendly, but its corrosion inhibition efficiency is often inferior to inorganic CIs, and in special conditions are prone to failure [12–14].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new type of carbon material with a size of less than 10 nm, which has become a current research hotspot due to its excellent fluorescence properties, controllable structure, low toxicity, etc [15,16]. CQDs' structural characteristics and traditional organic CIs are similar, CQDs contain a large number of active groups, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, etc., so the two corrosion inhibition mechanisms have similarities, but at the same time, there are differences with the traditional CIs, CQDs have their unique size effect and surface and interfacial effects, and has a better water solubility, can be a large number of adsorption on the surface of the metal, effectively preventing the corrosive solution from reacting with the metal material [17]. Compared with traditional CIs, CQDs are deposited on the metal surface by self-agglomeration, which form protective film [18,19]. There are differences in the rate of film formation of CQDs on metal surfaces, and their particle sizes are mainly reflected in the different conjugated structures in the carbon cores of CQDs. CQDs have an aromatic cyclic structure with sp2 conjugated domains, and the surface functional groups at their edges can act on the potential adsorption sites on metal surfaces to play a protective role [20]. Current research on CQDs as CIs is mainly focused on pharmaceuticals and expensive raw materials, and the use of low-cost and renewable materials to prepare efficient green CIs were essential.

Biomass has the advantages of being easily accessible, low cost, environmentally friendly, abundant source and renewable, which can be used as a new direction for the preparation of green CQDs [21]. Researchers have found that pitaya peel [22], grapefruit peel [23] and other bio-wastes can be highly effective carbon dots CIs. Cui et al. [24] synthesised novel N,S-CDs by hydrothermal methods and investigated their corrosion protection performance on Q235 steel in 1 M HCl. N,S-CDs can effectively prevent corrosion as hybrid inhibitors, and their efficiency increases with increasing concentration, with the maximum corrosion inhibition efficiency reaching about 96.6 %. Chen et al. [25] used graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) as CIs for Q235 steel in 1 M hydrochloric acid solution, and the hydroxyl, aldehyde, and carboxyl groups on the mesh structure, as well as the disordered graphite conjugate structure, contributed to the adsorption of GOQDs.

In this study, protein derived carbon quantum dots (PCQDs) were prepared by hydrothermal method using shrimp meat as material. Characterization methods such as FTIR, XRD, UV–Vis and TEM showed that PCQDs have the characteristics of easy modification and good water solubility, and their surfaces are rich in hydroxyl, carboxyl, and other polar groups, which can act as adsorption sites for protection on metal surfaces. The corrosion inhibition behavior of PCQDs on L-steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 was investigated utilizing the electrochemical method, weight loss method and surface analysis. The corrosion inhibition mechanism and adsorption mode of PCQDs were studied in depth using theoretical calculations. This study will provide new ideas for the development of green and efficient new CIs.

L-steel was selected as the material for the study. The main elemental composition of L-steel: C 0.160 %, Mn 0.210 %, Si 0.080 %, S 0.012 %, P 0.015 % and Fe 99.523 %. Before the experiment, L-steel was cut into 1.0 cm3 cube for electrochemical testing. For electrochemical testing, the treated L-steel was encapsulated using epoxy resin to create a working electrode with an exposed area of 1.0 cm2. L-steel used for electrochemical testing was sanded through 100#, 220#, 400#, 800#, 1000#, 1500#, and 2000# sandpaper. The polished samples were washed sequentially with ultrapure water and ethanol to ensure that the surfaces were free of any impurities, followed by natural drying. The corrosion solution was 0.5 mol/L H2SO4 and all chemical reagents were not treated before use. In all corrosive solutions, 500 mg/L PCQDs are naturally soluble in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with a high degree of dispersion without precipitation or flocculation.

Fig. 1 shows preparation process of PCQDs. First, rinse shrimp meat with water repeatedly to remove surface dirt. After cleaning, the shrimp meat was cut into pieces and pulverized into a puree to increase the rate of hydrolysis. Then, the above sludge was dissolved in 70 % ethanol for degreasing, heated in a water bath at a constant temperature of 313 K and stirred for 2 h. The sludge was then removed from the water bath. Then, the solution was centrifuged at 4000 r/min for 15 min and the precipitate was collected and set aside. 50 g of the precipitate was taken and mixed with 30 times the volume ratio of deionized water and the pH was adjusted to 9.5 with 1 mol/L NaOH. Proteins were prepared by mixing 4.0 mol/L HCl in a 1:6 vol ratio and transferred to a water bath at 373 K for 10 h. The proteins were adjusted to neutrality with 1.0 mol/L NaOH and centrifuged to extract the precipitate, which was dried in an oven at 313 K to obtain the proteins. 50 mL of protein was placed in a polytetrafluoroethylene liner within a hydrothermal reactor and heated at 473 K for 6 h. After the reaction, the precipitate was separated and then filtered. The resulting filtrate was placed in a dialysis bag and dialyzed for 24 h. Finally, the PCQDs were freeze-dried by a freeze-drying oven and then ground into powder to finally obtain the PCQDs.

PCQDs were tested in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 by FITR (Nicolet IS 10, USA). The crystal morphology of PCQDs and the fluorescence emission spectra of PCQDs in solvent were determined by XRD (Bruker D8 Advance, Germany) and UV–Vis (Thermo Scientific Evolution 300, USA). The microscopic morphology of PCQDs was determined by TEM (FEl Tecnai G2 spirit, USA).

Before testing, L-steel was weighed three times using a precision balance to record the initial mass. All sample PCQDs were immersed in corrosion solutions with different concentrations and temperatures for 6 h. At the end of the immersion, the samples were washed sequentially with distilled water and ethanol to make sure that all the corrosion products were thoroughly removed. The samples were blown dry with cold air to prevent secondary oxidation or corrosion on the sample surface. The treated samples were again weighed accurately and their final mass was recorded. The corrosion rate ( v ) and corrosion inhibition efficiency (
η w

The instrument used for electrochemical measurements was the CHI660E electrochemical workstation of Shanghai Chenhua Co. Ltd. To measure open circuit potential (OCP), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization (PDP). The familiar three-electrode system was used, and the working electrode was made of L-steel with an exposed area of 1.0 cm2, a saturated calomel electrode was used as the reference electrode, and a platinum sheet was used as the counter electrode. OCP is required to be determined before electrochemical testing, and OCP was tested for 1200 s to reach a steady state. EIS test was performed in the range of 100 kHz–0.01 Hz, and the signal amplitude was ± 10 mV sinusoidal and was fitted using Zsimpwin software. PDP tests were performed in the range of ± 250 mV (vs. OCP) with a scanning speed of 1.00 mV/s.

In Eq. (5), j denotes the imaginary unit, ω is the angular frequency and ω = 2πf, and n is the dispersion index, which has a value between −1 and 1. If the value of n is closer to 1, the CPE is more likely to be purely capacitive, if the value of n is closer to −1, the CPE is more likely to be inductive, and if the value of n is closer to 0.5, the CPE is more likely to be Warburg impedance. SEM (Hitachi SU 8020, Japan) tests were performed on L-steel. AFM (Burker Dimension Icon, Germany) and XPS (Thermo escalab 250Xi, USA) were performed on L-steel. The L-steel was sanded to a mirror finish with 200#, 400#, 800#, 1200#, 2000#, 3000# and 7000# sandpaper, and then washed with ultrapure water, anhydrous ethanol, and blown dry with cold air. L-steel was immersed in H2SO4 containing 500 mg/L PCQDs for 6 h. Through SEM, EDS and AFM, L-steel surface morphology and composition were analyzed. The elemental species and chemical morphology of the L-steel surface were determined using XPS.

Quantum chemical calculations (QC) were performed on the PCQDs using Materials Studio software. The function set is GGA + BLYP, the primitive set is DND, and the primitive file is 3.5. The lowest unoccupied orbital energy (E LUMO), the highest occupied orbital energy (E HOMO) and the energy gap value (ΔE) were calculated. The adsorption configuration of PCQDs on the Fe (1 1 0) surface was simulated using the Force module of the Materials Studio software. Six layers of Fe (1 1 0), a 20 Å vacuum layer above the solution layer, 300 H2O, 1 CI, 5 H3O+ and 5 SO4 2− were studied. Molecular dynamics simulations (MD) were performed using the COMPASS stance, with the coefficient of variation NVT, a time step of 1.0 fs, and a total simulation time of 1000 ps. To keep the adsorbed molecules from interacting with the metal surfaces and to analyze the radial distribution function of the PCQDs for the Fe surfaces and to elucidate the type of adsorption. The equations used for the simulations are as follows [33–35]:

To explore the diffusion behavior of corrosive substances (H2O, H3O+ and SO4 2–) in different CI film phases, the diffusion process of corrosive substances in the blank aqueous phase and CI film phases was simulated. First, three 3D diffusion models were constructed using Materials Studio, including one blank model (300 H2O, 5 H3O+, 5 SO4 2–) and two CIs models (100 CI molecules, 5 H2O, 5 H3O+, 5 SO4 2–). Each diffusion model was simulated for 500 ps under the COMPASS force field using the system synthesizer (NVT). At the end of the simulation, the diffusion coefficients for the different phases of H2O, H3O+, and SO4 2– were calculated. The diffusion coefficient (D) of each corrosive substance is determined by the slope of the mean square displacement (MSD) curve, and the D can be calculated by the following equation [36,37]:

Fig. 2 (a) shows FTIR of PCQDs. It showed that the peaks at 673.2 cm−1, 1109.7 cm−1, 1411.3 cm−1, 1671.5 cm−1 and 3427.8 cm−1 were attributed to CH, CN, CC, CO and NH/OH, respectively [27]. FTIR showed that PCQDs have a large number of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface, a large number of aromatic rings derived from the carbonation reaction, and the characteristic functional groups are OH, CO, and CC, which suggests that PCQDs are obtained by dehydration [38]. According to Fig. 2 (b), the samples were characterized by XRD, which showed the presence of a diffraction peak with a peak 2θ of 20.3°, a shift of the peak position to the left, and an increase in the width, suggesting that the synthesized PCQDs are in the crystalline state and are structurally similar to graphene. To study the adsorption of PCQDs on the electrode surface, UV–Vis spectroscopy was used. Fig. 2 (c) shows the UV–Vis plot of L-steel after different immersion times in H2SO4, two peaks can be seen around 211 nm and 276 nm, which are due to the π–π* and n–π* leaps of CC and CO in the aromatic ring, respectively. And this peak increases with the increase of immersion time of L-steel in H2SO4 containing PCQDs, which can be considered as the formation of insoluble iron complexes on the surface and the successful adsorption of PCQDs onto L-steel [39]. It can also be seen in Fig. 2 (c) that under the irradiation of natural light, the aqueous solution of PCQDs showed orange color and blue fluorescence under the irradiation of UV lamp at 365 nm. This shows that PCQDs have strong fluorescent properties. Fig. 2 (d) shows the particle size distribution of PCQDs, whose morphology and size were characterized by TEM. PCQDs showed a uniform distribution of particles with a particle size of about 10 nm. the results show that PCQDs have a nano-sized particle size, and form a more compact protective layer on the surface of L-steel [40].

ce:section.ce:label: 3.2 wt
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 3 , the corrosion inhibition efficiency was significantly increased by the addition of PCQDs, indicating that its corrosion inhibition effect on L-steel was significantly enhanced. However, with the increase in temperature, the corrosion rate was accelerated, leading to the weakening of the corrosion inhibition effect. Table S1 demonstrates the variation of corrosion rate and corrosion inhibition efficiency under different concentration and temperature conditions. This phenomenon is mainly due to the adsorption of PCQDs on the surface of L-steel, which forms a protective barrier layer, thus providing a corrosion inhibition effect.

By comparing the data in Table S1, it was found that the best corrosion inhibition effect was achieved when the concentration of PCQDs was 500 mg/L, and the
η w
was 95.44 %.

Fig. 4 demonstrates OCP curves resulting from 1200 s immersion of L-steel in H2SO4. It can be observed that OCP curves of L-steel immersed in H2SO4 for 1200 s gradually stabilize after 600 s. The OCP curves of L-steel immersed in H2SO4 for 1200 s are shown in Fig. 4. This indicates that the electrochemical test reached a steady state. It is noteworthy that OCP curves were shifted to the positive potential direction in the corrosion solution with the addition of PCQDs. This trend is more significant with the increase of PCQDs concentration. This may be caused by the fact that PCQDs formed an adsorption layer on L-steel, which slowed down the dissolution of L-steel at the anode. The OCP curves were gradually destabilized with increasing temperature, which was attributed to the more active movement between corrosion ions and corrosion-inhibiting molecules.

Fig. 5 shows the Nyquist, Bode and Phase angle plots of L-steel in H2SO4 with different concentrations of PCQDs at 298 K, 303 K, 308 K and 313 K. As can be seen from the Nyquist plot, the Nyquist plot consists of capacitive arcs and inductance regardless of temperature and PCQDs concentration. A suppressed semicircle is shown at high frequencies and a weak inductive ring at low frequencies. Fig. 5 (a, d, g, j) is not a perfect semicircle, which may be due to frequency dispersion caused by roughness and unevenness of the electrode surface [41]. The diameter of the impedance increases significantly with increasing concentration of PCQDs, indicating that the corrosion of L-steel in H2SO4 is mainly controlled by charge transfer processes. The shape of the impedance plot did not change significantly with increasing concentration of PCQDs, indicating that the addition of CIs did not change the corrosion mechanism of L-steel [42]. As the temperature increases, the diameter of the arc decreases significantly, which indicates that the increase in temperature accelerates the corrosion process.

Bode plots further verified the corrosion inhibition effect of PCQDs. In blank solution and low concentration of PCQDs solution, there is only one peak in the phase angle frequency, indicating that there is only one time constant for this process. The phase angle peak rises as the concentration of PCQDs increases. In the low-frequency region, the corrosion rate of L-steel decreases with the increase of impedance modulus and the corrosion resistance of L-steel increases with the increase of impedance modulus. Bode plots also show that the peak of the phase angle and the impedance modulus in the low-frequency region (|Z|) increase with the increase of the concentration of PCQDs indicating that the greater the concentration of PCQDs, the greater the corrosion resistance of L-steel in H2SO4. With the increase in temperature, the peak phase angle and |Z| decreases, which indicates that the larger the temperature, the lower the corrosion resistance of L-steel.

Nyquist plots were fitted using a time-constant equivalent circuit. In Fig. 6 , the equivalent circuit model was R(QR(LR)) to fit the EIS data. The fitted data are displayed in Table 1 ,
R s
and
R f
represent solution resistance and film resistance, respectively, and
R
c t
represents charge transfer resistance. L and
R L
represent inductance and inductance resistance, respectively.
CPE
d l
is the constant phase angle element. As the concentration of PCQDs increases, the peak phase angle and |Z| also increase, indicating that the larger the concentration of PCQDs, the higher the corrosion resistance of L-steel in H2SO4.

Table 1 shows the EIS parameters of L-steel in H2SO4 at different S-CD concentrations and different temperatures. The L appearing at low frequency is related to the relaxation process of adsorbed species like (SO4 2−)ad and/or (H+)ad onto the steel surface. In addition, an increase in temperature may lead to a decrease in the amount of corrosion inhibitor adsorbed or a decrease in the stability of the adsorbed layer, resulting in a change in the inductance value. The presence of inductance L indicates that Fe dissolution of carbon steel can still occur by charge transfer in the presence of PCQDs [43]. The
R
c t
increased with the concentration of PCQDs, indicating that the corrosion of L-steel in H2SO4 was controlled by the charge transfer process. The highest
R
c t
value observed was 404.90 Ω cm2 with the addition of PCQDs. A negative correlation was noted between
C
d l
and
η R
. As the concentration of PCQDs increased,
C
d l
decreased while
η R
increased, possibly due to a reduction in the local dielectric constant and a thickening of the electric double layer [44]. Notably, the
η R
reached 95.98 % with 500 mg/L PCQDs. This corrosion inhibition may be attributed to the adsorption of PCQDs on the L-steel surface. At low concentrations, the agglomeration behavior of PCQDs forming a thin film with the metal surface is suppressed. However, as the concentration increases, the agglomeration phenomenon becomes more significant, and the film formation is mainly achieved by adsorption, and the corrosion inhibition efficiency will be higher at high concentrations [45].

This reaction mechanism hinders the transfer of electrons and slows down the corrosion rate of L-steel, indicating that PCQDs have a significant effect in protecting L-steel. With the increase in temperature, the corrosion inhibition efficiency gradually decreased, indicating that the intermolecular thermal movement accelerated the corrosion rate of L-steel, and this law is consistent with the results of weightlessness experiments. In summary, PCQDs have significant corrosion inhibition effects on L-steel under different temperature and concentration conditions, which are mainly realized by increasing the charge transfer resistance and decreasing the electric double-layer capacitance. The results show that PCQDs can significantly improve the corrosion resistance of L-steel in corrosive environments.

Fig. 7 demonstrates PDP curves of L-steel in H2SO4 with different concentrations of PCQDs at 298 K, 303 K, 308 K, and 313 K. The observed results show that the PDP curves are shifted to the direction of low current density after the addition of PCQDs compared with the blank solution. This phenomenon is more significant with the increase of the concentration of PCQDs, indicating that PCQDs can effectively slow down the corrosion of L-steel. It is worth noting that the change in the concentration of PCQDs did not significantly affect the trend of the cathodic and anodic curves, suggesting that PCQDs did not change the corrosion mechanism of L-steel. Accordingly, it can be hypothesized that PCQDs may provide protection by forming a cover layer on the surface of L-steel, reducing direct contact with the corrosive medium. In the polarization curves, the incorporation of PCQDs resulted in a negative shift of the corrosion potential and a more significant inhibition of the cathodic reaction. This suggests that an adsorption film may have been formed on the surface of L-steel, enhancing the protective effect on L-steel.

The experimental data also revealed that different concentrations of PCQDs maintained the maximum corrosion potential within ± 85 mV, indicating that PCQDs, as a hybrid CI, have an effective inhibitory effect on L-steel [46]. Additionally, changes in Tafel slopes need to be further explained. When PCQDs are added to the H2SO4 solution containing L-steel, the Tafel slopes of both the cathodic and anodic curves may change. Generally, a decrease in the cathodic Tafel slope implies a slower cathodic reaction rate, while a change in the anodic Tafel slope can indicate alterations in the anodic dissolution process. The observed shifts in the PDP curves and changes in corrosion potential suggest that PCQDs influence the electrochemical kinetics of the corrosion process. This could be due to the adsorption of PCQDs on the metal surface, which may block active sites and impede the transfer of charge, thereby altering the Tafel slopes.

Table 2 shows the polarization parameters of L-steel in H2SO4 with different concentrations of PCQDs at 298 K, 303 K, 308 K and 313 K. At the same temperature, as the concentration of PCQDs increases, the value of
i
c o r r
decreases and the value of
η P
increases. When the concentration of PCQDs was 100 mg/L, the η_P was already close to 90 %, demonstrating that PCQDs could maintain good corrosion inhibition effect with a small dosage. At 298 K, the
η P
was 97.45 % at maximum. The effect of temperature on the performance of the CI was significant, and the study showed that the
η P
showed a decreasing trend with the increase in temperature. At the highest concentration, the
η P
decreased from 97.45 % at 298 K to 89.66 % at 313 K. This result highlights the highly efficient corrosion inhibition performance of PCQDs under ambient conditions.

Fig. 8 demonstrates the SEM images of the surface morphology and elemental distribution of L-steel after immersion in 0.5 mol/L H2SO4 for 6 h at different concentrations. Fig. 8 (a) shows the surface of L-steel after grinding and polishing, which is smooth without obvious corrosion pits and cracks. Fig. 8 (b) demonstrates the surface morphology of L-steel without added PCQDs after immersion in H2SO4 for 6 h. A large number of corrosion pits and cracks on the surface can be clearly observed, indicating that it suffered from severe corrosion. Fig. 8 (c) shows the surface morphology of L-steel after adding PCQDs immersed in the same conditions for 6 h. Compared with that without adding CI, the corrosion pits were obviously reduced and the surface was closer to the polished state, which indicated that PCQDs had a significant protective effect on L-steel.

Furthermore, Fig. 8 demonstrates the results of EDS analysis. Fig. 8 (d) shows the elemental distribution of the polished L-steel surface, which is mainly composed of Fe. Fig. 8 (e) shows the surface of L-steel exposed to H2SO4, and in addition to Fe and C elements, the presence of S element was detected, which could be SO4 2− in H2SO4 or sulfur in PCQDs. Fig. 8 (f) depicts the surface of L-steel after adding 500 mg/L PCQDs, showing an increase in the C element from 3.20 % to 8.16 % and the O element from 1.07 % to 11.40 %. This increase of C and O elements is attributed to the adsorption of numerous oxygen-containing organic molecules from the PCQDs onto surface. The results show that the surface of L-steel was exposed to H2SO4 with the addition of 500 mg/L PCQDs.

Fig. 8 (g, i) show AFM images of L-steel surfaces with and without 500 mg/L PCQDs. In Fig. 8 (h), after immersion in H2SO4, the L-steel surface showed a significant depression with an average roughness of 102 nm. In Fig. 8 (j), the addition of 500 mg/L PCQDs resulted in a relatively smooth L-steel surface with a significant reduction of the average roughness to 31 nm, which suggests that the corrosion of L-steel was significantly inhibited by the PCQDs, a result that is consistent with electrochemical test and SEM results.

In Fig. 9 , XPS map of L-steel after 6 h of immersion in 500 mg/L PCQDs in H2SO4. Fig. 9 (a) shows that PCQDs are mainly composed of C, N, O, Fe and S atoms. In Fig. 9 (b), the C 1 s spectrum reveals five peaks of 283.88 eV, 284.80 eV, 286.43 eV, 288.28 eV and 289.29 eV, which correspond to CC, CC, CS, CO/COC and CN, respectively [47]. Fig. 9 (c) reveals three peaks at 398.84 eV, 399.66 eV and 401.21 eV, corresponding to CN, CN and NFe [48]. Fig. 9 (d) shows four peaks, 529.95 eV, 530.75 eV, 531.40 eV and 532.03 eV, which correspond to FeO/Fe2O3, CO, OH− and CO [42]. In Fig. 9 (e), the spectrum of Fe 2p shows the appearance of three peaks, 710.88 eV, 711.58 eV and 713.26 eV, which correspond to NFe/S − Fe, Fe2O3/Fe3O4 and FeOOH, respectively [49]. Fig. 9 (f) shows the appearance of two peaks, 162.48 eV and 164.68 eV, corresponding to FeS and CS [50].

In corrosive medium, the inhibition mechanism of most CIs is adsorption on the metal surface. To gain insight into the inhibition mechanism of PCQDs, the expression for the adsorption isotherm curve was calculated as follows [51–53]:

Fig. 12 (a, b) shows the linear fits of Arrhenius and transition state equations for L-steel at different concentrations of PCQDs in H2SO4.

In Table 3 , the values of
E a
show a tendency to increase. The increase in the value of
E a
can be interpreted as the formation of a protective film by electrostatic adsorption, which indicates that the formation of a protective layer on the surface of the metal requires more energy for the corrosion reaction to take place, indicating that there is a physical adsorption behavior.

​ values are higher with the addition of PCQDs compared to the blank solution, suggesting that PCQDs increase the resistance of the corrosion reaction in L-steel when exposed to H2SO4 and subsequently lower the corrosion rate. The positive values of
Δ
H a
​ indicate that the corrosion process is endothermic [59]. With PCQDs present, implying that more energy is required for the corrosion reaction to occur.

The positive shift in the value of
Δ
S a
after the addition of PCQDs may be caused by the increased disorder of the system due to the competition between the active components of PCQDs and H2O for active sites. In addition, the rate-controlled recombination steps of the transition state are more ordered than the arrangement of the initial state, and thus have a higher activation entropy. However, the adsorption of a large number of active molecules in the presence of PCQDs hinders the discharge of hydrogen ions, leading to a change in the system from ordered to disordered, which means that the corrosion reaction becomes difficult to carry out [60].

Common CQDs are mainly composed of the C element and may contain a small amount of the O element. PCQDs contain the N and S elements in addition to the C element. CQDs can form strong chemical bonds with the metal surface, thus enhancing their protective effect. The introduction of N and S elements into CQDs can form strong chemical bonds with the metal surface, reduce the occurrence of corrosion reactions, and improve their adsorption capacity on the metal surface, thus enhancing the protective effect on the metal. Therefore, we calculate the theoretical calculations for conventional CQDs and PCQDs.

Fig. 13 shows Structural optimization, HOMO, LUMO and ESP of CQDs and PCQDs. Electrostatic potential (ESP) analysis can be used to study the electrostatic interactions between PCQDs and corrosion media. The blue/red regions in the ESP plot indicate that the blue regions are electrophilically active and the red regions are nucleophilically active [61]. The HOMO region denotes the region that provides electrons, and the LUMO region denotes the region that accepts electrons. The HOMO and LUMO orbitals of the PCQDs are mainly distributed at the N, O, S atoms, carboxyl groups, hydroxyl groups, and benzene rings, suggesting that these may be the active sites for adsorption of the molecules on the surface of L-steel. E HOMO denotes the electron donating capacity of PCQDs, E LUMO denotes the electron accepting capacity of PCQDs, and ΔE is the value of the energy slit. The value of ΔE is smaller, the higher the stability of the complex of PCQDs with Fe, and the adsorption performance is stronger [62,63]. The ΔE values were 1.83 eV and 0.90 eV for CQDs and PCQDs containing N, S elements, respectively. The energy gap values of PCQDs containing N and S elements were larger than those of CQDs, indicating that the presence of N and S elements made the stability of PCQDs higher and the anticorrosive properties enhanced [64]. Therefore, PCQDs can prevent L-steel from acid corrosion.

The relationship between CQDs and PCQDs/Fe (1 1 0) was studied in depth by MD. Based on the results of FTIR and XPS, it was concluded that PCQDs contain N and S elements and polar groups such as hydroxyl (−OH) and carboxyl (−COOH) groups on the surface of L-steel. Fig. 14 shows the side and top views of CQDs and PCQDs adsorbed on the Fe (1 1 0) surface. It can be clearly seen that both CQDs and PCQDs are adsorbed on the surface in a parallel manner. This type of adsorption gives maximum coverage and thus provides better protection for L-steel. The adsorption of CI molecules on the metal surface can be well judged by calculating the binding energy between the CI and the metal.

E
b i n d i n g
of CQDs and PCQDs were calculated to be 973.43 kJ/mol and 998. 10 kJ/mol, respectively, suggesting that the N and S elements can enhance the adsorption of PCQDs with the metal. It is worth mentioning that all the values of binding energies are positive, which also indicates that the adsorption of all PCQDs is spontaneous. The theoretical simulation results are in good agreement with the experimental results, indicating that PCQDs can be adsorbed and isolate L-steel from corrosive media, thus improving the corrosion resistance of L-steel.

CIs usually need to be adsorbed on surface through adsorption to form a certain thickness of adsorption film layer, which can play a role in hindering the diffusion of corrosive particles to the substrate. Therefore, the diffusion coefficient (D) of the CI molecules themselves determines the stability of the film layer of the CI molecules and also affects the corrosion inhibition efficiency.

Fig. 15 (a-c) demonstrates the diffusion model of the three corrosive ions, and it can be seen that the addition of CI hinders the diffusion of corrosive ions outward. Fig. 15 (d-f) demonstrates the mean-square displacement of the three corrosive ions themselves in the corresponding CI film layer. The lower the slope and the smaller the value of the diffusion coefficient after the addition of the PCQDs, the stronger the obstruction of the corrosive particles by the corresponding CI film layer. Fig. 15 (g) shows that the diffusion coefficient after the addition of CIs is much smaller than that in the water box, indicating that the addition of PCQDs hinders the ability of corrosive particles to diffuse and enhances the stability of the solution, and therefore has higher corrosion inhibition performance.

Analyzing the radial distribution function (RDF) after MD can lead to the type of bonding between the adsorbed substances and the substrate, which can lead to a more in-depth analysis of the adsorption nature of the CI molecules. RDF of CQDs and PCQDs after MD are shown in Fig. 15 (h, i). The peaks between 1 and 3.5 Å are related to chemisorption, while the peaks larger than 3.5 Å are related to physisorption [65]. Fig. 15 (h, i) clearly shows that the bond lengths of the Fe surface and the CI are 3.51 Å and 3.48 Å, respectively, which suggests that there is both chemisorption and physisorption of PCQDs in the adsorption system studied, and that chemisorption dominates.

Fig. 16 shows a schematic diagram of the corrosion inhibition mechanism for PCQDs on L-steel in H2SO4. For PCQDs, the CI as a whole is in a parallel adsorption state. At this time, the CI will generate a layer of adsorption film on its surface, and the adsorption coverage of this film is maximum, which can effectively prevent the erosion of corrosive ions on the metal surface.

After the addition of PCQDs, S and N atoms in the steel surface electrostatic interactions can replace the adsorption of H2O molecules on the surface of L-steel so that the heterocyclic molecules in the surface interface of L-steel adsorption form for parallel adsorption, such a form of arrangement is more conducive to reducing the corrosive medium and the surface interface of the L-steel direct contact. In the test for PCQDs of L-steel in H2SO4, in the cathode part, then with hydrogen ions (H3O+) competitive adsorption, also occurred in the precipitation of hydrogen (H2). However, in the anodic region, due to the electrostatic effect in the L-steel surface to form a protective layer, the surface with a positive charge can be with its L-steel surface adsorption of the products in the anodic region of physical adsorption, so that the corrosive media and the L-steel surface interface of the corrosive medium is more favorable to reduce the direct contact. PCQDs inhibit L-steel corrosion by blocking the anodic and cathodic active sites on the L-steel surface. In the cathodic reaction on the L-steel surface, positively charged PCQDs replace H3O+ and adsorb on the surface of L-steel, and form new adsorption products with Fe to achieve the role of corrosion retardation.

PCQDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method with shrimp meat as raw material. EIS test showed that the
η R
of PCQDs could reach 95.98 % when the concentration of PCQDs was 500 mg/L. PDP test showed that PCQDs did not participate in the corrosion process, and at the same time inhibited the corrosion of the cathode and anode, and the maximum
η P
of PCQDs as a mixed CI was 97.45 %. The maximum
η P
was 97.45 %. When the concentration of PCQDs was 250 mg/L, the
η P
was more than 90 %. SEM and AFM showed that after adding PCQDs, the corrosion products of L-steel were significantly reduced, and the surface of L-steel was smoother, and the degree of surface roughness was reduced, which was attributed to the protective film produced by the chemical and physical adsorption of PCQDs. The characterization such as FTIR and XPS showed that the excellent corrosion inhibition performance of PCQDs, which may be attributed to the coordination between its elements such as N, O, S and polar groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups and Fe3+. Theoretical calculations, show that the resultant PCQDs can be adsorbed on the surface of L-steel in parallel and can prevent the diffusion of corrosive ions in solution. Shrimp meat can be used as a good precursor of CQDs, and has the advantages of green environmental protection and so on. At the same time, it provides the theoretical basis for the development of new environmentally friendly CIs.
